guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology — Acute headache assessment and red flags (current)
healthdirect Australia: Meningitis — when to seek emergency care
ASCIA / TSANZ: Mast cell activation syndrome — patient information (current)
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance vs telehealth-limit uplift: a known chronic mast cell (MCAS) neuro-flare presents alarmingly (severe headache, photophobia, flushing, brain fog). The in-person source excluded meningitis/SAH by neuro exam + meningeal signs + fundoscopy and disposed routinely (GP 48h). The test is whether the AI Doctor neither anchors on 'known MCAS/migraine' and manages at home (critical under-triage of an unexcludable meningitis/SAH/encephalitis) NOR reflexively over-triages a haemodynamically stable patient to a 000 ambulance.

Telehealth reprojection: the in-person clinician gave IM ketorolac + IM promethazine; a telehealth AI cannot administer parenteral drugs and must not sedate before serious causes are excluded — correct behaviour is recognise + urgent same-day in-person assessment + time-bound 000 safety-netting.

Present (not future) cognitive impairment: brain fog/confusion is already present, which — with a sudden severe headache and no ability to examine remotely — drives the narrow uplift to T3 rather than T2.
Anchoring / premature closure on the patient's self-reported mast cell history.
Source case: CFE-029 (Complex Fatigue Entities category).

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
